title: Matrix Arithmetic
summary: Matrix addition, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication with full rules and worked examples.
content_html:
Matrix Addition and Subtraction

Matrices of the same size can be added/subtracted element-by-element:

[1  2] + [5  6] = [6   8]
[3  4]   [7  8]   [10  12]

Scalar Multiplication

Multiply every element by the scalar k:

3 × [1  2] = [3  6]
    [3  4]   [9  12]

Matrix Multiplication

For A (m×n) and B (n×p), the product AB is m×p. The (i,j) entry of AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B:

(AB)ᵢⱼ = Σₖ aᵢₖ · bₖⱼ

Example:

A = [1  2]    B = [5  6]
    [3  4]        [7  8]

AB = [1·5+2·7  1·6+2·8] = [19  22]
     [3·5+4·7  3·6+4·8]   [43  50]

Key Properties

  • Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative: AB ≠ BA in general.
  • It is associative: (AB)C = A(BC).
  • It is distributive: A(B+C) = AB + AC.
  • AI = IA = A (identity matrix acts as multiplicative identity).
  • A·0 = 0·A = 0 (zero matrix).

Dimensions Must Be Compatible

A is m×n and B is p×q: product AB exists only if n = p. The result is m×q.

(2×3)(3×4) → valid, result is 2×4
(2×3)(4×3) → INVALID (3 ≠ 4)

Powers of Matrices

A² = AA, A³ = AAA, etc. (only defined for square matrices).

exercises.title: Compatibility check
exercises.prompt: Can you multiply a 2×3 matrix by a 3×5 matrix?

exercises.title: Matrix product entry
exercises.prompt: For AB, the (i,j) entry is computed as:
options: Dot product of row i of A with column j of B
Sum of all entries in row i
exercises.answer: Dot product of row i of A with column j of B

exercises.title: Result size
exercises.prompt: If A is 4×2 and B is 2×3, then AB has size:

exercises.title: AI = A
exercises.prompt: For any matrix A and compatible identity matrix I:

exercises.title: Matrix addition requirement
options: A and B have the same dimensions
Number of columns of A equals number of rows of B
exercises.answer: A and B have the same dimensions
